interactions.summary: Jiang introduces Pericles’ funeral oration, then a student reads the passage offering comfort rather than condolence and urging parents to have more children for the state.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang setup followed by student reading quoted Thucydides/Pericles passage.

interactions.summary: Jiang interprets the funeral oration as imperial logic: dead sons become useful because they died for empire, orphaned children become future soldiers, and Pericles’ equilibrium strategy creates plague in Athens.
Jiang frames the Melian Dialogue as rebellious islands trying to leave the Athenian war, then a student begins reading the Athenian argument that they will not use moral pretenses.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang setup followed by student reading Thucydides.

speaker_notes.note: Quoted funeral oration passage read aloud by a student; segment 67 is a pronunciation correction.
Beginning of quoted Melian Dialogue read aloud by a student.

claims.claim: He interprets Pericles’ funeral oration as a speech about empire rather than democracy.
Pericles’ rhetoric converts dead sons and orphaned children into resources for imperial reproduction.
Pericles’ plague strategy results from trying to preserve balance rather than decisively invading Sparta.
Jiang introduces the Melian Dialogue as evidence that Athens coerced allies and islands that wanted out of its war.

signature_moments.moment: Pericles tells grieving parents the empire can redeem useless sons by turning their deaths and orphans into more soldiers.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the lecture’s darkest contrast between democratic rhetoric and imperial reproduction.

signature_moments.moment: Put everyone inside the walls to preserve strategy, and the city manufactures its own plague.
signature_moments.source_phrase: guess what happens if you put a lot of people in one place

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Pericles’ famous speech, interpreted by Jiang as imperial rather than democratic rhetoric.
Thucydides passage Jiang uses to show Athenian empire speaking openly in coercive terms.

chronology_notes.note: The packet moves from Pericles’ wartime rhetoric and plague to the Melian Dialogue as Athens’ imperial language becomes explicit.

uncertainty_notes.note: Quoted-source readings contain ASR errors, but Jiang’s interpretation around them identifies the passages.
